name: Coordination Compounds
subject: Chemistry

sections.heading: Werner's Theory and Basic Definitions
sections.body:
A coordination compound contains a central metal atom or ion bonded to ligands by coordinate (dative) bonds, retaining its identity in solution. Examples: [Cu(NH3)4]SO4, K4[Fe(CN)6], [Co(NH3)6]Cl3.

Werner's postulates (1893):

  1. Metals have two kinds of valencies: primary (ionisable) = oxidation state, and secondary (non-ionisable) = coordination number.
  2. Primary valencies are satisfied by negative ions; secondary valencies are satisfied by ligands (neutral or charged).
  3. Secondary valencies are directional ‚ÄĒ they give the complex a definite geometry.

Key terms:

  • Coordination sphere: metal + ligands inside [...]; behaves as a single unit.
  • Counter ion: outside the brackets; ionises in solution.
  • Coordination number (CN): number of ligand atoms directly bonded to the metal.
  • Donor atom: the atom of the ligand that donates the electron pair (e.g. N in NH3).

sections.heading: Ligands ‚ÄĒ Types
sections.body:
A ligand is a Lewis base donating an electron pair. By denticity:

  • Monodentate: 1 donor (Cl‚ĀĽ, H2O, NH3, CN‚ĀĽ, CO).
  • Bidentate: 2 donors (ethylenediamine "en" H2N‚ÄďCH2‚ÄďCH2‚ÄďNH2; oxalate C2O4¬≤‚ĀĽ).
  • Polydentate: more than 2 (EDTA‚Āī‚ĀĽ is hexadentate).
  • Chelating ligand: bidentate or polydentate that grips the metal in a ring ‚ÄĒ produces extra stability (chelate effect).
  • Ambidentate: ligand with two different donor atoms but only one binds at a time (NO2‚ĀĽ vs ONO‚ĀĽ; SCN‚ĀĽ vs NCS‚ĀĽ).

Strong-field ligands (CN‚ĀĽ, CO, NO2‚ĀĽ, en) cause large d-orbital splitting and electron pairing ‚Üí low spin. Weak-field ligands (I‚ĀĽ, Br‚ĀĽ, F‚ĀĽ, H2O) cause small splitting ‚Üí high spin. Spectrochemical series: I‚ĀĽ < Br‚ĀĽ < S¬≤‚ĀĽ < SCN‚ĀĽ < Cl‚ĀĽ < F‚ĀĽ < OH‚ĀĽ < H2O < NH3 < en < NO2‚ĀĽ < CN‚ĀĽ < CO.

sections.heading: IUPAC Nomenclature
sections.body:
Rules:

  1. Cation is named first, then anion (whether the complex is cation or anion).
  2. Ligands are named alphabetically (ignoring numerical prefixes), then the metal.
  3. Anionic ligands end in -o (chloro, hydroxo, cyano, sulfato, oxalato).
  4. Neutral ligands keep their names except H2O = aqua, NH3 = ammine, CO = carbonyl, NO = nitrosyl.
  5. Use di-, tri-, tetra- for simple ligands; bis-, tris-, tetrakis- if the ligand name itself contains a numerical prefix (e.g. bis(ethylenediamine)).
  6. Oxidation state of the metal is given in Roman numerals in parentheses immediately after the metal name.
  7. If complex is an anion, metal name ends in -ate (often using Latin: ferrate for Fe, cuprate for Cu, plumbate for Pb, argentate for Ag, aurate for Au).

Examples:

  • [Cu(NH3)4]SO4 = tetraamminecopper(II) sulfate.
  • K4[Fe(CN)6] = potassium hexacyanidoferrate(II).
  • [Co(NH3)4Cl2]Cl = tetraamminedichloridocobalt(III) chloride.
  • [Pt(NH3)2Cl2] = diamminedichloridoplatinum(II).

sections.heading: Isomerism in Coordination Compounds
sections.body:
Structural isomerism:

  • Linkage: ambidentate ligand binds via different atoms ‚ÄĒ [Co(NH3)5(NO2)]¬≤‚Āļ vs [Co(NH3)5(ONO)]¬≤‚Āļ.
  • Coordination: ligands swap between cation and anion in [Co(NH3)6][Cr(CN)6] vs [Cr(NH3)6][Co(CN)6].
  • Ionisation: counter ion swaps with a ligand ‚ÄĒ [Co(NH3)5SO4]Br vs [Co(NH3)5Br]SO4.
  • Solvate (hydrate): water inside vs outside coordination sphere ‚ÄĒ [Cr(H2O)6]Cl3 (violet), [Cr(H2O)5Cl]Cl2¬∑H2O (blue-green), [Cr(H2O)4Cl2]Cl¬∑2H2O (dark green).

Stereoisomerism:

  • Geometrical (cis‚Äďtrans): in square planar [MA2B2] (e.g. cisplatin = cis-[Pt(NH3)2Cl2], an anti-cancer drug; trans is inactive). Octahedral [MA4B2] and [M(AA)2B2] also show cis‚Äďtrans.
  • Optical (d‚Äďl): octahedral [M(AA)3] (e.g. [Co(en)3]¬≥‚Āļ) and cis-[M(AA)2X2] are chiral. Tetrahedral with four different ligands also shows enantiomers.

sections.heading: Valence Bond Theory (VBT)
sections.body:
VBT explains shape and magnetism in terms of hybridisation of the metal orbitals that accept electron pairs from ligands.

CNHybridisationGeometry
2spLinear
4sp¬≥Tetrahedral
4dsp¬≤Square planar
5sp¬≥d / dsp¬≥Trigonal bipyramidal
6d¬≤sp¬≥ (inner orbital, low spin)Octahedral
6sp¬≥d¬≤ (outer orbital, high spin)Octahedral

Examples: [Ni(CN)4]¬≤‚ĀĽ ‚ÄĒ dsp¬≤, diamagnetic; [NiCl4]¬≤‚ĀĽ ‚ÄĒ sp¬≥, paramagnetic; [Fe(CN)6]¬≥‚ĀĽ ‚ÄĒ d¬≤sp¬≥, low spin (1 unpaired e‚ĀĽ); [FeF6]¬≥‚ĀĽ ‚ÄĒ sp¬≥d¬≤, high spin (5 unpaired e‚ĀĽ).

Limitations of VBT: doesn't quantitatively explain colour, magnetism near borderline cases, or thermodynamic stability; doesn't predict relative stabilities of geometries.

sections.heading: Crystal Field Theory (CFT)
sections.body:
CFT treats the metal‚Äďligand bond as electrostatic. In an isolated atom the five d orbitals are degenerate. Approach of ligands lifts the degeneracy.

Octahedral field: d orbitals split into t2g (dxy, dyz, dxz; lower) and eg (dz¬≤, dx¬≤‚ąíy¬≤; higher). Splitting őĒo (or 10Dq).

Tetrahedral field: opposite splitting ‚ÄĒ e (lower) below t2 (higher). őĒt ‚Čą (4/9) őĒo; small splitting means tetrahedral complexes are almost always high-spin.

Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE): net energy stabilization when d electrons fill the split levels. For octahedral, CFSE = (‚ąí0.4¬∑nt2g + 0.6¬∑neg) őĒo.

High vs Low Spin (octahedral, d‚Āī‚Äďd‚Ā∑): if őĒo > pairing energy P, electrons pair up in t2g ‚Üí low spin (strong-field ligands like CN‚ĀĽ, CO). If őĒo < P ‚Üí high spin (weak-field ligands like H2O, F‚ĀĽ).

Colour explanation: visible light promotes a d electron from t2g to eg (őĒo falls in visible range). The complementary colour is observed. d‚Āį and d¬Ļ‚Āį ions are colourless.

sections.heading: Metal Carbonyls and Bonding
sections.body:
Metal carbonyls: complexes of metals with CO, all electrically neutral with metal in zero oxidation state. Examples: Ni(CO)4 (tetrahedral), Fe(CO)5 (trigonal bipyramidal), Cr(CO)6 (octahedral), Mn2(CO)10, Co2(CO)8.

The M‚ÄďCO bond has dual character: (i) ŌÉ donation from filled C lone pair into empty metal d orbital; (ii) ŌÄ back-donation from filled metal d orbital into empty antibonding ŌÄ* of CO. This synergic bonding strengthens the M‚ÄďC bond and weakens the C‚Č°O bond (lower IR stretching frequency than free CO).

sections.heading: Stability of Complexes and Applications
sections.body:
Stability constant (formation constant) K: M + nL ‚áĆ MLn; K = [MLn]/([M][L]n). Larger K means a more stable complex. Stability is favoured by:

  • High charge on metal ion;
  • Small size of metal ion;
  • Greater basicity of ligand;
  • Chelating effect (chelates much more stable than monodentate analogues).

Importance of coordination compounds:

  • Biological: chlorophyll (Mg¬≤‚Āļ porphyrin), haemoglobin (Fe¬≤‚Āļ porphyrin), vitamin B12 (Co¬≥‚Āļ corrin).
  • Medicine: cisplatin (anticancer); EDTA in lead/heavy metal poisoning chelation therapy.
  • Analytical chemistry: EDTA titration for water hardness; Tollen's, Fehling's reagents.
  • Metallurgy: extraction of Ag and Au by cyanide leaching ([Ag(CN)2]‚ĀĽ); purification of Ni by Mond's process via Ni(CO)4.
  • Catalysis: Wilkinson's catalyst RhCl(PPh3)3 for hydrogenation.

summary: Coordination compounds contain a central metal bonded to ligands through coordinate bonds. Werner's primary (ionisable) and secondary (non-ionisable, directional) valencies became today's oxidation state and coordination number. Ligands are classified by denticity and as strong/weak field per the spectrochemical series (CN‚ĀĽ > CO > en > ... > F‚ĀĽ > Cl‚ĀĽ > Br‚ĀĽ > I‚ĀĽ). IUPAC names are built systematically (cation first, ligands alphabetical, oxidation state in Roman numerals, anionic complexes get -ate). Isomerism includes structural (linkage, ionisation, coordination, hydrate) and stereoisomerism (cis‚Äďtrans and optical). VBT explains shape via hybridisation; CFT explains colour, magnetism, and high/low spin via d-orbital splitting (őĒ_o, őĒ_t = 4/9 őĒ_o). Coordination compounds are central in biology (chlorophyll, haemoglobin), medicine (cisplatin, EDTA chelation), metallurgy (Au/Ag extraction, Mond), and catalysis.
pitfalls: Naming ligands non-alphabetically ‚ÄĒ alphabetise by ligand name, ignoring numerical prefixes.
Forgetting Latin -ate names for anionic complexes: ferrate (Fe), cuprate (Cu), argentate (Ag).
Confusing primary and secondary valency ‚ÄĒ primary = oxidation state, secondary = coordination number.
Saying [Ni(CN)_4]^{2‚ąí} is tetrahedral ‚ÄĒ it is square planar (dsp¬≤, low spin, diamagnetic).
Predicting tetrahedral complexes as low-spin ‚ÄĒ they are almost always high-spin (őĒ_t too small).
Forgetting that CFSE doesn't include pairing energy term explicitly ‚ÄĒ add it back when comparing low-spin states.
Treating ŌÄ back-donation as ionic ‚ÄĒ it is covalent and synergic with ŌÉ donation.
